title: Check yourself: text & content
questions.0.q: Someone wants two lines of text one below the other. They press Enter between them in the code. They run it — the text comes out glued on one line. What have they missed?
questions.0.options.0: They needed a second Enter — one is not enough
questions.0.options.1: The browser ignores blank lines in the code; separation must be asked for with a tag
questions.0.options.2: The editor broke the code when saving
questions.0.hint: Ask yourself who the blank lines in the code are for — your eyes, or the browser?
questions.0.explain: Blank lines in code are for your eyes. The browser swallows them on purpose — otherwise every reshuffle of the code would shift the page. Want something separate? Say it with a tag.
questions.1.q:
They want "Price: 25 leva" on one line. They put the label in one

, the amount in another

, and glue them together with no blank line at all. They still split. Why?
questions.1.options.0:
Because

is a block tag — each takes a whole line, however short it is
questions.1.options.1: Because there is no space between them
questions.1.options.2: Because they must be inside one <h1>
questions.1.hint:
The split does not come from how they typed it. It comes from the tag itself. What kind of tag is

?
questions.1.explain: Two blocks will never stand side by side — this is not a matter of nudging. The fix is ONE paragraph, with an inline tag inside it for the part that should stand out.
questions.2.q:
They want some air between two paragraphs. They stack three
in a row. It works. What is wrong with it?
questions.2.options.0: Nothing — if it looks right, it is right
questions.2.options.1: does not mean "gap", it means "the line ends here but the thought does not". Gaps are CSS
questions.2.hint: It works and it looks right. That is exactly why it is a trap. Ask what the tag SAYS, not what it does.
questions.2.explain:
You pick the tag by meaning.
is for an address, a verse, a line of a song — not for air between paragraphs.
questions.3.q: They are running a sale. They want the old price crossed out because "it looks nicer and shows up grey". What is the better reason to use <del>?
questions.3.options.0: That <del> makes the text grey and struck through
questions.3.options.1: That <del> means "this was here and no longer applies" — it carries meaning, not a look
questions.3.options.2: That <del> is shorter to type than CSS
questions.3.options.3: That without <del> the price will not show
questions.3.hint: Tomorrow you change the style. Which of the two reasons survives that?
questions.3.explain: If you pick a tag for its look, the meaning vanishes the day you change the style. <del> says what HAPPENED to that price. How it looks is CSS.
questions.4.q:
They write on their page: "Today I learned the

tag". They run it — the sentence comes out mangled and the tag is nowhere. What happened?
questions.4.options.0: The browser cannot tell "here is a tag" from "I am talking about a tag" — it executed it
questions.4.options.1:
The

tag cannot be written in lowercase
questions.4.hint: What does an angle bracket mean to the browser? Does it have any way of knowing you are only talking about a tag this time?
questions.4.explain: To the browser an angle bracket means one thing: a tag begins. So when you want to SHOW the symbol, you write its name: < and >. Code cannot describe itself using itself.
questions.5.q: A three-line address: street, floor, city. Which is right?
questions.5.options.0:
Three separate

— each line stands on its own
questions.5.options.1: A list — the lines are an enumeration
questions.5.options.2:
One

with
inside — one address, on new lines
questions.5.hint: How many thoughts is an address — one or three? And can you reorder the street and the city?
questions.5.explain:
An address is one thought, not three. And it is not a list — you cannot reorder the street and the city. That is exactly what
is for: a new line INSIDE one paragraph.
questions.6.q: They want the word "important" bold, because it is important. One colleague says: "use <strong>". Another says: "use CSS". Who is right?
questions.6.options.0: The first — the word truly IS important, and <strong> carries exactly that meaning
questions.6.options.1: The second — bold is always styling
questions.6.options.2: Neither — bold is not done in HTML
questions.6.options.3: It makes no difference, as long as it comes out bold
questions.6.hint: Both give you thick letters. The question is not how it will look, but why it is bold.
questions.6.explain: The question is not "should it be bold" but "why". If the word CARRIES weight — <strong>. If you just want thick letters for looks — CSS. Same appearance, two different reasons. And remember: the screen reader is silent about both. So important things get said in words too, not only in bold.
